title: Hello World
description:
Write a Python program that prints the text `Hello, World!` to the console.

**Expected Output:**
```
Hello, World!
```

**💡 Hint:** In Python, you can display text using `print()`. Put your text inside quotes within the parentheses.

starterCode.python: # Use the print() function to display Hello, World!

title: Print Your Name
description:
Write a Python program that prints `My name is CodeByte` to the console.

**Expected Output:**
```
My name is CodeByte
```

**💡 Hint:** Use `print()` with a string inside double quotes `"..."` or single quotes `'...'`. The text inside the quotes is printed exactly as written.

starterCode.python: # Print the sentence: My name is CodeByte

title: Print Multiple Words
description:
Use a single `print()` statement with **multiple arguments** to print `I love Python` to the console.

**Expected Output:**
```
I love Python
```

**💡 Hint:** You can pass multiple values to `print()` separated by commas. For example, `print("A", "B")` prints `A B` — Python automatically adds a space between them.

starterCode.python:
# Use print() with multiple arguments separated by commas
# Example: print("word1", "word2", "word3")

title: Print Two Lines
description:
Write a program that prints two lines of output:
- Line 1: `Hello`
- Line 2: `World`

**Expected Output:**
```
Hello
World
```

**💡 Hint:** Each `print()` statement automatically moves to a new line after printing. So calling `print()` twice will produce two separate lines.

starterCode.python: # Use two print() statements — one for each line

title: Print with Custom Separator
description:
Use the `sep` parameter in `print()` to print `2025-04-12` by passing `2025`, `04`, and `12` as separate arguments.

**Expected Output:**
```
2025-04-12
```

**💡 Hint:** By default, `print()` separates arguments with a space. You can change this using `sep="..."`. For example, `print("A", "B", sep="-")` prints `A-B` instead of `A B`.

starterCode.python:
# Use print() with the sep parameter
# Syntax: print(arg1, arg2, arg3, sep="your_separator")

title: Print a Pattern
description:
Write a program that prints this star triangle pattern:

```
*
**
***
```

**💡 Hint:** Use three separate `print()` statements. You can repeat a character using string multiplication: `"*" * 3` gives you `***`. Or simply type the stars directly inside quotes.

starterCode.python:
# Print a star triangle — each line has one more star
# You can use "*" * number to repeat stars

title: What Does This Print? — Comma Arguments
description:
What will the following code output?

```python
print("Hello", "World")
```

**💡 Hint:** When `print()` receives multiple arguments separated by commas, it joins them with a space by default.

title: What Does This Print? — String Concatenation
description:
What will the following code output?

```python
print("Code" + "Byte")
```

**💡 Hint:** The `+` operator between two strings joins them directly together — no space is added automatically. This is called *concatenation*.

title: What Does This Print? — Sep Parameter
description:
What will the following code output?

```python
print("a", "b", "c", sep="-")
```

**💡 Hint:** The `sep` parameter controls what character goes *between* the printed values. By default it's a space, but here it's been changed.

title: What Does This Print? — End Parameter
description:
What will the following code output?

```python
print("Hello", end=" ")
print("World")
```

**💡 Hint:** The `end` parameter controls what comes *after* the printed text. By default it's a newline `\n`, but here it's been changed to a space — so the next `print()` continues on the same line.
